# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: Hiking is a way I enjoy doing different things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies—practical things. I wear layers, use hiking poles, boots, and a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight, just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fit, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax. We usually have periods of chatting and periods of silence to take it all in.

Interviewer: What’s the difference between hiking alone and with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings more. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction, but also quiet moments.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, not in winter.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: Usually a couple of hours. This year I’ve been busier, so about two to three hours, but in past years I’ve done longer hikes, sometimes six hours or more.

Interviewer: How many hikes do you do in a year?

Participant: Probably 20 to 30, mostly from spring to fall.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half and half. I revisit some favorite spots yearly but also look for new places.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same places?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and many are close by. I like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress as I get stronger.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better; each has unique qualities.

Interviewer: How do you approach safety when hiking alone?

Participant: I take precautions. I only hike trails I’ve done with a group before, avoid unfamiliar or long trails, and stick to well-traveled paths where I’ll see other people. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family, so they know where I am and when to expect me back.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use other apps or devices to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Any other devices you use on hikes?

Participant: No, just my phone and Fitbit.

Interviewer: What’s your main reason for using the camera?

Participant: To capture nice views, interesting patterns, animals, or plants I want to paint later. Also, if I see a plant I don’t know, I take a picture to identify it later.

Interviewer: How do pictures help you connect back to places you’ve hiked?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of when and where I was. Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just writing it down.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other devices besides your phone and Fitbit?

Participant: Those cover my needs. I’m not sure what else is out there, but I’d try something new if it could improve my experience.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, do you think you could hike alone safely?

Participant: Sort of. I’d stick to shorter, well-populated places and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel as confident going farther without location sharing.

Interviewer: What are your preferences for hike difficulty?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles. In summer, a typical hike is six or seven miles. I enjoy shorter hikes too, but if I have a full day, I prefer longer ones. I consider eight miles or more a long hike.

Interviewer: Regarding your recent hike, I want to share your trail map. Is that okay?

Participant: Yes, that’s fine.

Interviewer: We noticed your Strava GPS started after you had already been on the trail for a while. Why didn’t you start it at the trailhead?

Participant: I’m not sure. I thought I did start it at the trailhead. Maybe I activated it late by mistake.

Interviewer: You didn’t use your device much during the hike. Why?

Participant: I didn’t need to check the map often because I’ve done the trail before. Also, it was warm that day, so we moved quickly and didn’t stop much for pictures.

Interviewer: When you checked the time, which device did you use?

Participant: Usually my Fitbit for just the time. I might see the time on my phone when I pick it up, but for quick checks, I use the Fitbit.

Interviewer: You took only two pictures on this hike. Why so few?

Participant: Mostly we were in the trees, so fewer views. I took pictures at a lookout point and of a plant I wanted to identify.

Interviewer: How does being in a natural environment far from human living feel?

Participant: I really enjoy it. There’s so much life—plants, animals, sounds, smells—that’s different from my daily experience. I notice a lot more about my surroundings.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown, but mostly I stay on them.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery. Did you use it on this hike?

Participant: No, I used it while driving back. My phone didn’t need charging during the hike.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone is old and drains quickly, especially on long hikes. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to navigate or call for help. I can also charge my flashlight with it.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for long ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record the hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of my device use. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes about what I see.

Interviewer: Did being conscious of device use distract you from your nature experience?

Participant: Probably somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband was using the same device, it didn’t feel weird. We reminded each other when to record. But sometimes it felt strange if other people saw me talking to myself.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay hiking?

Participant: Yes, especially on this hike because my husband had an errand. We had to watch the time to get back on schedule.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Yes, unfortunately.

Interviewer: Do you think it’s possible to hike without checking the time and still get back safely?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time when you have an appointment might be more stressful. But I get a better experience when I don’t have to worry about time. Sometimes I check the time to gauge the heat of the day rather than to plan my return.

Interviewer: Why did you use the map on a trail you know well?

Participant: The trail is less used and overgrown in parts, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS connection, so I check the map to keep it recording.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating. Thank you.

Interviewer: Thank you. We’ll reach out when the gift card is ready. Have a good evening.

Participant: You too. Goodbye.

Interviewer: Goodbye.

## Summary
**Short:** The participant views hiking as a restorative outdoor activity that benefits both physical fitness and mental well-being. They prepare carefully with practical gear and use technology like Strava and a Fitbit for safety and tracking, balancing social interaction and solitude on trails. Seasonal changes and familiar versus new trails enrich their experience, while device use sometimes distracts but also encourages mindfulness. Time awareness can detract from relaxation but is necessary for scheduling.

**Long:** The participant describes hiking as a multifaceted activity that promotes physical health, mental clarity, and creative inspiration, especially through photography for later painting. They emphasize thorough preparation with layered clothing, food, and safety devices, including phone apps like Strava for location sharing and a Fitbit for monitoring. Hiking alone allows deeper reflection and nature observation, while group hikes provide social bonding with alternating quiet moments. They hike frequently in spring to fall, revisiting familiar trails to observe seasonal changes and personal progress, while also exploring new locations. Safety is prioritized by choosing well-known, populated trails and sharing GPS data with family. Device use, while helpful for navigation and documentation, sometimes interrupts immersion in nature, and checking time can reduce relaxation, though it remains necessary for managing commitments. Overall, hiking is a cherished, balanced practice that integrates technology, social connection, and solitude to support well-being.

## Themes
1. **Connection with Nature and Mental Well-being** (high) — Hiking is valued for its mental health benefits, providing relaxation, calmness, and a refreshing break from daily stress, along with a deep appreciation of natural surroundings.
    - "I enjoy being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise."
    - "Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking."
    - "I really enjoy it. There’s so much life—plants, animals, sounds, smells—that’s different from my daily experience."

2. **Social Interaction vs. Solitude** (medium) — Hiking experiences differ when done alone versus with others; solo hikes encourage more reflection and noticing surroundings, while group hikes provide social bonding but sometimes distract from nature.
    - "When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings more."
    - "With friends, there’s more social interaction, but also quiet moments."
    - "Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax."

3. **Use of Technology and Devices** (high) — Technology like phones, cameras, Fitbit, and apps (Strava) are used for safety, tracking, capturing memories, and navigation, though device use can sometimes distract from the natural experience.
    - "I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate."
    - "I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family, so they know where I am and when to expect me back."
    - "It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record."
    - "Probably somewhat, yes."

4. **Safety and Preparation** (medium) — Safety is a priority, especially when hiking alone, involving carrying practical supplies, sticking to known or well-traveled trails, and using technology for location sharing.
    - "I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies—practical things."
    - "I only hike trails I’ve done with a group before, avoid unfamiliar or long trails, and stick to well-traveled paths where I’ll see other people."
    - "I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family."

5. **Hiking Frequency, Duration, and Preferences** (high) — The participant hikes frequently (20-30 times per year, mostly spring to fall), prefers challenging hikes when time allows, and balances revisiting favorite spots with exploring new locations.
    - "Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, not in winter."
    - "Probably 20 to 30, mostly from spring to fall."
    - "I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles."

6. **Seasonal Appreciation and Environmental Respect** (medium) — The participant values seasonal changes for their unique qualities and prefers staying on established trails to preserve the environment and for safety.
    - "I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better; each has unique qualities."
    - "I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety."

## Quote Highlights
- **Memorable**: "Hiking is a way I enjoy doing different things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise."
  - Rationale: This quote captures the participant's holistic and personal definition of hiking as both a physical activity and mental escape from daily stress and technology.
  - Suggested Use: Use in introductory sections of research or presentations to highlight the multifaceted value of hiking.
- **Emotional**: "Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking."
  - Rationale: Expresses the emotional and mental benefits of hiking, emphasizing feelings of calmness and renewed energy.
  - Suggested Use: In mental health or wellness studies to illustrate hiking's positive psychological impact.
- **Behavioral**: "I take precautions. I only hike trails I’ve done with a group before, avoid unfamiliar or long trails, and stick to well-traveled paths where I’ll see other people. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family, so they know where I am and when to expect me back."
  - Rationale: Details concrete safety behaviors and use of technology to enable safe solo hiking.
  - Suggested Use: In safety guidelines or app design to reflect real user practices and needs.
- **Memorable**: "When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings more. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction, but also quiet moments."
  - Rationale: Highlights the nuanced difference between solo and group hiking experiences, balancing mindfulness and social connection.
  - Suggested Use: For user experience research or storytelling about different hiking modes.
- **Behavioral**: "Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just writing it down."
  - Rationale: Describes a specific behavior of using photography and digital tools to enhance memory and connection with nature.
  - Suggested Use: In design of digital tools or apps supporting memory and reflection for outdoor activities.
- **Emotional**: "It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of my device use."
  - Rationale: Reveals the participant’s awareness of technology use during hiking and its subtle impact on attention and experience.
  - Suggested Use: To inform design of less intrusive tracking devices or studies on technology’s impact on nature experiences.
- **Emotional**: "I really enjoy it. There’s so much life—plants, animals, sounds, smells—that’s different from my daily experience. I notice a lot more about my surroundings."
  - Rationale: Expresses a deep emotional connection and sensory appreciation of the natural environment.
  - Suggested Use: In environmental education or campaigns promoting nature immersion.
- **Behavioral**: "I carry an extra battery because my phone is old and drains quickly, especially on long hikes. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to navigate or call for help."
  - Rationale: Highlights practical preparation behavior reflecting safety awareness and reliance on technology.
  - Suggested Use: In gear recommendations or safety training for hikers.

## Follow-up Questions
### Clarifying
- You mentioned sometimes hiking without your phone or listening to podcasts when alone. Can you clarify how often you do this and what influences that choice?
- When you said you usually bring an extra battery pack for your phone or flashlight, can you clarify if you carry both devices or just one at a time?
- You said you use Strava to share your GPS location with family. Can you clarify if you keep this sharing active throughout the entire hike or only during certain parts?
- You mentioned that on your recent hike your Strava GPS started after you had already been on the trail for a while. Can you clarify how long the delay was before you started recording?
### Probing
- You explained that hiking helps you get physically fit and mentally refreshed. Can you describe a specific moment on a hike when you felt this mental refresh most strongly?
- You said you enjoy revisiting favorite spots to see how the hike changes with the seasons and to track your own progress. Can you elaborate on how you track your progress and what changes you notice?
- You mentioned that using the recording device made you think about keeping a hike journal or notes. What kind of notes or journal entries would you consider making, and how might that enhance your experience?
- You talked about feeling a bit distracted by checking the time and device use during hikes. Can you explore what strategies you might try to reduce this distraction while still staying safe and on schedule?
### Contrast / Compare
- You described hiking alone as a time for more reflection and noticing surroundings, versus social hiking with chatting and quiet moments. Can you compare how these two experiences affect your overall sense of relaxation?
- You said you prefer established trails for safety and environmental reasons, but sometimes trails are overgrown. How does hiking on overgrown parts compare to well-maintained trails in terms of enjoyment and challenge?
- You mentioned that without apps like Strava you would feel less confident hiking alone. How does your confidence and safety perception differ between using technology and hiking without it?
- You noted that checking the time can distract from wellness but is sometimes necessary for appointments. Can you contrast a hike where you didn’t check the time at all with one where you had to monitor it closely, in terms of your experience?

## Quality Review
- Status: pass
- Severity: none

# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but usually the topics aren’t stressful, so it still helps me relax. We also have quiet periods during hikes, so it balances out.

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction.

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, from spring to fall, about 20 to 30 times a year.

Participant: These days, about two to three hours. In past years, I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you handle safety concerns?

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use other apps or devices to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Why do you use the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture nice views, interesting patterns, or animals so I can remember them and paint later. Also, to identify plants by taking pictures and looking them up afterward.

Interviewer: How do these pictures help you connect back to your hikes?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of where and when I was. Looking back at pictures helps me recall the experience more vividly than just writing notes.

Participant: Yes, that’s fine.

Participant: I’m not sure. I thought I started it at the trailhead. Maybe I activated it late by mistake.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown, but I avoid going off-trail.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old, so for longer hikes, I might need it. Also, as a precaution in case of emergencies, to keep my phone or flashlight charged.

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Participant: The trail is less used and overgrown in parts, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS signal, so I check to ensure it’s recording.

## Summary
**Short:** The participant values hiking as a way to relax, connect with nature, and improve physical and mental health. They prepare carefully with essential gear and use technology like Strava and a Fitbit for safety and tracking. Hiking alone allows for deeper reflection, while group hikes offer social bonding. They prefer established trails, enjoy seasonal changes, and balance device use to avoid distraction from the natural experience.

**Long:** The participant describes hiking as a meaningful activity that promotes physical fitness, mental calmness, and creative inspiration, often capturing photos for later painting. Preparation includes bringing adequate supplies and safety tools, with technology such as Strava for location sharing and a Fitbit for health monitoring playing key roles in enhancing safety and tracking progress. They hike frequently, favoring both familiar and new trails, and appreciate the unique qualities each season brings. While hiking alone fosters introspection and heightened awareness of surroundings, group hikes provide valuable social interaction. The participant carefully manages device use to minimize distractions, acknowledging that time checks can sometimes detract from the immersive nature experience but are necessary when scheduling demands arise. They prioritize established trails for environmental preservation and safety, and their approach reflects a thoughtful balance between enjoying nature and leveraging technology for comfort and security.

## Themes
1. **Physical and Mental Health Benefits** (high) — Hiking is valued for improving physical fitness and cardiovascular health, as well as providing mental refreshment and relaxation.
    - "It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health."
    - "Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking."

2. **Use of Technology for Safety and Connection** (high) — The participant relies on apps like Strava and devices such as a Fitbit and phone camera for safety, tracking, and enhancing the hiking experience.
    - "I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family and tell them when I expect to return."
    - "I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate."

3. **Social vs. Solo Hiking Experiences** (medium) — Hiking alone allows for more reflection and noticing surroundings, while hiking with friends emphasizes social interaction and bonding.
    - "When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better."
    - "I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond."

4. **Preparation and Safety Precautions** (medium) — The participant prepares carefully with food, water, clothing layers, and extra battery packs, and chooses familiar, well-traveled trails for safety.
    - "I bring enough food, water, first aid supplies, layers of clothes, hiking poles, boots, and a backpack."
    - "I take precautions by only hiking trails I know well, usually ones I’ve done with a group before."

5. **Impact of Time Awareness on Hiking Experience** (medium) — Checking time and managing schedules can detract from the relaxation and immersion in nature, but is sometimes necessary for planning.
    - "Checking the time distracts from the wellness you seek in nature."
    - "I get a better experience when I don’t have to worry about time."

6. **Seasonal and Environmental Appreciation** (medium) — Each season offers unique hiking experiences, and the participant appreciates the variety of plants, animals, and changing trail conditions.
    - "I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences."
    - "It’s alive with plants and animals, different sounds and smells."

## Quote Highlights
- **Emotional**: "I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise."
  - Rationale: This quote reveals the participant's emotional motivation for hiking as a form of mental relaxation and escape from daily stress.
  - Suggested Use: Use in messaging or campaigns emphasizing hiking as a mental health and stress-relief activity.
- **Behavioral**: "I bring enough food, water, first aid supplies, layers of clothes, hiking poles, boots, and a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight, just in case."
  - Rationale: Details the participant’s careful preparation habits, demonstrating safety-conscious and practical hiking behavior.
  - Suggested Use: In educational materials or apps promoting safe hiking preparation and gear checklists.
- **Emotional**: "Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking."
  - Rationale: Highlights the positive mental health benefits the participant gains from hiking.
  - Suggested Use: To support research or content on nature’s role in mental wellness and recovery.
- **Behavioral**: "When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction."
  - Rationale: Describes how hiking behavior and experience differ when solo versus with others.
  - Suggested Use: To inform design of hiking apps or experiences tailored for solo versus group users.
- **Memorable**: "Looking back at pictures helps me recall the experience more vividly than just writing notes."
  - Rationale: Emphasizes the memorable impact of visual documentation on recalling hiking experiences.
  - Suggested Use: For designing photo journaling or memory prompts in hiking or travel apps.
- **Emotional**: "Checking the time distracts from the wellness you seek in nature."
  - Rationale: Expresses the emotional tension between time awareness and immersion in the natural experience.
  - Suggested Use: To explore design solutions that minimize time-related stress during outdoor activities.
- **Behavioral**: "I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate."
  - Rationale: Shows integration of technology into hiking for both creative and fitness tracking purposes.
  - Suggested Use: To guide development of multi-functional hiking apps combining fitness tracking with creative engagement.
- **Behavioral**: "I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles. On a day off, I prefer longer hikes—eight miles or more. Otherwise, six or seven miles is typical."
  - Rationale: Provides insight into the participant’s preferences regarding hike difficulty and duration.
  - Suggested Use: For segmenting users by hiking intensity preferences in outdoor activity planning tools.

## Follow-up Questions
### Clarifying
- You mentioned that sometimes when hiking with friends, you focus more on conversation and might miss some sights. Can you clarify how often this happens and whether it changes your overall hiking experience?

# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies. I wear layers, use hiking poles, boots, and carry a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other tasks afterward.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax. We usually have periods of chatting and periods of silence to take it all in.

Interviewer: How does hiking alone compare to hiking with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I go without my phone, other times I listen to podcasts or talks.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, usually spring to fall, not in winter.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: Typically two to three hours this year, though in past years I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: How many hikes do you do annually?

Participant: Probably 20 to 30 hikes a year, mostly on weekends from spring to fall.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half are familiar places I visit yearly, and half are new locations I try to explore.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same hiking spots?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and proximity is a factor since they’re close by. I also like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress over time.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you manage safety concerns?

Participant: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know from previous group hikes. I avoid new or unfamiliar trails alone and stick to shorter, well-traveled paths where I’m likely to see others. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family so they know where I am and when to expect me back.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use any other apps to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera a lot to capture scenes or plants I want to paint or identify later. I also use a Fitbit to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Do you use any other devices on hikes?

Participant: No, just my phone and Fitbit.

Interviewer: What is your main purpose for using the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture beautiful views, interesting patterns, animals, or plants I want to identify or paint later.

Interviewer: How do your photos help you connect back to the places you’ve hiked?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of when and where I was. Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just written notes.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other hiking devices?

Participant: My phone and Fitbit cover my needs, and I’m not sure what else is available. If I learn about something useful, I might try it.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, do you think you could hike safely alone?

Participant: Somewhat. I’d probably stick to shorter, well-populated trails and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel as confident going farther without location tracking.

Interviewer: What are your preferences regarding hike difficulty?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time availability is the biggest factor. If I have a full day, I enjoy longer hikes—up to about 16.5 miles. In summer, a typical hike is around six or seven miles. Shorter hikes are enjoyable too, but longer ones are preferred when possible.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown and require some bushwhacking, but mostly I stay on the path.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery. Did you use it on your last hike?

Participant: No, I didn’t need it on the hike but used it while driving back.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old. On longer hikes, I might need to recharge. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to keep my phone or flashlight powered.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record your hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of how I used it. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes, which could be interesting.

Interviewer: Did using the device take away from your experience in nature?

Participant: Somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband was using the same device, it felt natural. We reminded each other when to record. It felt a bit strange when other people saw me talking to myself, though.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay hiking?

Participant: Yes. On the last hike, we had to get back for an errand, so we checked the time to make sure we left in time.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Unfortunately, yes.

Interviewer: Could you hike without checking the time and still enjoy it?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time when you have an appointment would be more stressful. But if I don’t have somewhere to be, I get a better experience without worrying about time. Sometimes I check the time just to be aware of the heat of the day.

Interviewer: You used the map a few times on a trail you know well. Why?

Participant: The trail is less used and sometimes overgrown, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS connection, so I check to ensure it’s recording properly.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating.

Interviewer: We appreciate your time. We’ll reach out when the gift card is ready.

Participant: Thank you. Have a good evening.

Interviewer: You too. Goodbye.

Participant: Bye.

## Summary
**Short:** The participant views hiking as a beneficial outdoor activity that promotes physical fitness, mental relaxation, and creative inspiration through photography. They prepare carefully with essential gear and technology like a phone, extra battery, and Fitbit, and balance hiking alone and with friends to enjoy both solitude and social bonding. They prefer established trails, appreciate seasonal changes, and use apps like Strava for safety and connection. While technology sometimes distracts, it also enhances their experience by helping track progress and preserve memories.

**Long:** The participant describes hiking as a multifaceted activity that supports their physical health, mental well-being, and artistic pursuits. They hike frequently, mostly on weekends from spring to fall, splitting time between familiar and new trails. Preparation includes layered clothing, food, first aid, hiking poles, and technology such as a phone with GPS and an extra battery, along with a Fitbit for fitness tracking. Hiking alone allows for reflection and close observation of nature, while hiking with friends offers social interaction without significantly detracting from relaxation. They prioritize safety by sticking to known trails, sharing GPS location via Strava, and avoiding unfamiliar paths alone. The participant values the seasonal variety in hiking experiences and prefers challenging hikes when time permits. Although technology like cameras and recording devices occasionally distracts from immersion in nature, it also aids in capturing memories and monitoring progress. Time awareness can sometimes limit the hiking experience, but the participant balances this with practical needs. Overall, hiking is a meaningful, well-prepared activity that blends physical exercise, mental refreshment, social connection, and creative expression.

# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, first aid supplies, layers of clothes, hiking poles, boots, and a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight, just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but usually the topics aren’t stressful, so it still helps me relax. We also have quiet periods during hikes, so it balances out.

Interviewer: What’s the difference between hiking alone and with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, from spring to fall, about 20 to 30 times a year.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: These days, about two to three hours. In past years, I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half and half. I revisit some favorite spots yearly but also look for new places.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same places?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and they’re usually close by. I like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress over time.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you handle safety concerns?

Participant: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know well, usually ones I’ve done with a group before. I stick to shorter, well-traveled trails where I’m likely to see other people. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family and tell them when I expect to return.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use other apps or devices to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Why do you use the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture nice views, interesting patterns, or animals so I can remember them and paint later. Also, to identify plants by taking pictures and looking them up afterward.

Interviewer: How do these pictures help you connect back to your hikes?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of where and when I was. Looking back at pictures helps me recall the experience more vividly than just writing notes.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other hiking devices?

Participant: The phone and Fitbit cover my needs. I’m not sure what else is out there, but I’d try something new if it seemed helpful.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, would you feel safe hiking alone?

Participant: Somewhat. I’d stick to shorter, busier trails and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel confident going far without location tracking.

Interviewer: What are your preferences for hike difficulty and length?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles. On a day off, I prefer longer hikes—eight miles or more. Otherwise, six or seven miles is typical.

Interviewer: Regarding your recent hike, I want to share your trail map. Is that okay?

Participant: Yes, that’s fine.

Interviewer: We noticed your GPS on Strava started after you had already been hiking for a while. Why?

Participant: I’m not sure. I thought I started it at the trailhead. Maybe I activated it late by mistake.

Interviewer: You didn’t use your device much during the hike. Why?

Participant: It’s a trail I’ve done several times, so I didn’t need to check the map often. Also, it was warm that day, so I moved quickly and didn’t stop much to take pictures.

Interviewer: When you check the time, do you use your Fitbit or phone?

Participant: Just the Fitbit if I’m only checking the time. I might see the time on my phone when I pick it up, but I prefer the watch for time checks.

Interviewer: You took only two pictures on this hike. Why so few?

Participant: Most of the hike was in the trees, so fewer photo opportunities. I took one at a lookout and another of a plant I wanted to identify.

Interviewer: How do you feel being in an environment far from human living spaces?

Participant: I really enjoy it. It’s alive with plants and animals, different sounds and smells. It’s very different from daily life, so I notice more about my surroundings.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown, but I avoid going off-trail.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery pack. Did you use it on this hike?

Participant: No, I used it on the drive back but didn’t need it during the hike.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old, so for longer hikes, I might need it. Also, as a precaution in case of emergencies, to keep my phone or flashlight charged.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record your hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less because I didn’t want to interrupt recording. It made me more conscious of device use. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes.

Interviewer: Did being conscious of device use detract from your nature experience?

Participant: Somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband used the same device, it felt normal. We reminded each other when to record. But it felt a bit strange talking to myself when passing other hikers.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay out hiking?

Participant: Yes, especially on this hike because my husband had an errand, so we needed to leave on time.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Yes, unfortunately.

Interviewer: Could you hike without checking the time and still enjoy the experience?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time with an appointment would be more stressful. But I get a better experience when I don’t have to worry about time. Sometimes I check time to gauge the heat of the day rather than to plan my return.

Interviewer: Why did you use the map on a trail you know well?

Participant: The trail is less used and overgrown in parts, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS signal, so I check to ensure it’s recording.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating. Thank you.

Interviewer: Thank you. We’ll be in touch. Have a good evening.

Participant: You too. Goodbye.

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: Hiking is a way I enjoy doing different things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies—practical things. I wear layers, use hiking poles, boots, and a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight, just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fit, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax. We usually have periods of chatting and periods of silence to take it all in.

Interviewer: What’s the difference between hiking alone and with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings more. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction, but also quiet moments.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, not in winter.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: Usually a couple of hours. This year I’ve been busier, so about two to three hours, but in past years I’ve done longer hikes, sometimes six hours or more.

Interviewer: How many hikes do you do in a year?

Participant: Probably 20 to 30, mostly from spring to fall.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half and half. I revisit some favorite spots yearly but also look for new places.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same places?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and many are close by. I like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress as I get stronger.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better; each has unique qualities.

Interviewer: How do you approach safety when hiking alone?

Participant: I take precautions. I only hike trails I’ve done with a group before, avoid unfamiliar or long trails, and stick to well-traveled paths where I’ll see other people. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family, so they know where I am and when to expect me back.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use other apps or devices to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Any other devices you use on hikes?

Participant: No, just my phone and Fitbit.

Interviewer: What’s your main reason for using the camera?

Participant: To capture nice views, interesting patterns, animals, or plants I want to paint later. Also, if I see a plant I don’t know, I take a picture to identify it later.

Interviewer: How do pictures help you connect back to places you’ve hiked?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of when and where I was. Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just writing it down.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other devices besides your phone and Fitbit?

Participant: Those cover my needs. I’m not sure what else is out there, but I’d try something new if it could improve my experience.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, do you think you could hike alone safely?

Participant: Sort of. I’d stick to shorter, well-populated places and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel as confident going farther without location sharing.

Interviewer: What are your preferences for hike difficulty?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles. In summer, a typical hike is six or seven miles. I enjoy shorter hikes too, but if I have a full day, I prefer longer ones. I consider eight miles or more a long hike.

Interviewer: Regarding your recent hike, I want to share your trail map. Is that okay?

Participant: Yes, that’s fine.

Interviewer: We noticed your Strava GPS started after you had already been on the trail for a while. Why didn’t you start it at the trailhead?

Participant: I’m not sure. I thought I did start it at the trailhead. Maybe I activated it late by mistake.

Interviewer: You didn’t use your device much during the hike. Why?

Participant: I didn’t need to check the map often because I’ve done the trail before. Also, it was warm that day, so we moved quickly and didn’t stop much for pictures.

Interviewer: When you checked the time, which device did you use?

Participant: Usually my Fitbit for just the time. I might see the time on my phone when I pick it up, but for quick checks, I use the Fitbit.

Interviewer: You took only two pictures on this hike. Why so few?

Participant: Mostly we were in the trees, so fewer views. I took pictures at a lookout point and of a plant I wanted to identify.

Interviewer: How does being in a natural environment far from human living feel?

Participant: I really enjoy it. There’s so much life—plants, animals, sounds, smells—that’s different from my daily experience. I notice a lot more about my surroundings.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown, but mostly I stay on them.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery. Did you use it on this hike?

Participant: No, I used it while driving back. My phone didn’t need charging during the hike.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone is old and drains quickly, especially on long hikes. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to navigate or call for help. I can also charge my flashlight with it.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for long ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record the hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of my device use. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes about what I see.

Interviewer: Did being conscious of device use distract you from your nature experience?

Participant: Probably somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband was using the same device, it didn’t feel weird. We reminded each other when to record. But sometimes it felt strange if other people saw me talking to myself.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay hiking?

Participant: Yes, especially on this hike because my husband had an errand. We had to watch the time to get back on schedule.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Yes, unfortunately.

Interviewer: Do you think it’s possible to hike without checking the time and still get back safely?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time when you have an appointment might be more stressful. But I get a better experience when I don’t have to worry about time. Sometimes I check the time to gauge the heat of the day rather than to plan my return.

Interviewer: Why did you use the map on a trail you know well?

Participant: The trail is less used and overgrown in parts, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS connection, so I check the map to keep it recording.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating. Thank you.

Interviewer: Thank you. We’ll reach out when the gift card is ready. Have a good evening.

Participant: You too. Goodbye.

Interviewer: Goodbye.

## Summary
**Short:** The participant views hiking as a restorative outdoor activity that benefits both physical fitness and mental well-being. They prepare carefully with practical gear and use technology like Strava and a Fitbit for safety and tracking, balancing social interaction and solitude on trails. Seasonal changes and familiar versus new trails enrich their experience, while device use sometimes distracts but also encourages mindfulness. Time awareness can detract from relaxation but is necessary for scheduling.

**Long:** The participant describes hiking as a multifaceted activity that promotes physical health, mental clarity, and creative inspiration, especially through photography for later painting. They emphasize thorough preparation with layered clothing, food, and safety devices, including phone apps like Strava for location sharing and a Fitbit for monitoring. Hiking alone allows deeper reflection and nature observation, while group hikes provide social bonding with alternating quiet moments. They hike frequently in spring to fall, revisiting familiar trails to observe seasonal changes and personal progress, while also exploring new locations. Safety is prioritized by choosing well-known, populated trails and sharing GPS data with family. Device use, while helpful for navigation and documentation, sometimes interrupts immersion in nature, and checking time can reduce relaxation, though it remains necessary for managing commitments. Overall, hiking is a cherished, balanced practice that integrates technology, social connection, and solitude to support well-being.

# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, and first aid supplies. I wear layers, use hiking poles, boots, and carry a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other tasks afterward.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and miss some sights, but often the topics aren’t related to my stress, so it still helps me relax. We usually have periods of chatting and periods of silence to take it all in.

Interviewer: How does hiking alone compare to hiking with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I go without my phone, other times I listen to podcasts or talks.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, usually spring to fall, not in winter.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: Typically two to three hours this year, though in past years I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: How many hikes do you do annually?

Participant: Probably 20 to 30 hikes a year, mostly on weekends from spring to fall.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half are familiar places I visit yearly, and half are new locations I try to explore.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same hiking spots?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and proximity is a factor since they’re close by. I also like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress over time.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you manage safety concerns?

Participant: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know from previous group hikes. I avoid new or unfamiliar trails alone and stick to shorter, well-traveled paths where I’m likely to see others. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family so they know where I am and when to expect me back.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use any other apps to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera a lot to capture scenes or plants I want to paint or identify later. I also use a Fitbit to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Do you use any other devices on hikes?

Participant: No, just my phone and Fitbit.

Interviewer: What is your main purpose for using the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture beautiful views, interesting patterns, animals, or plants I want to identify or paint later.

Interviewer: How do your photos help you connect back to the places you’ve hiked?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of when and where I was. Looking back at pictures helps me remember the experience more vividly than just written notes.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other hiking devices?

Participant: My phone and Fitbit cover my needs, and I’m not sure what else is available. If I learn about something useful, I might try it.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, do you think you could hike safely alone?

Participant: Somewhat. I’d probably stick to shorter, well-populated trails and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel as confident going farther without location tracking.

Interviewer: What are your preferences regarding hike difficulty?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time availability is the biggest factor. If I have a full day, I enjoy longer hikes—up to about 16.5 miles. In summer, a typical hike is around six or seven miles. Shorter hikes are enjoyable too, but longer ones are preferred when possible.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown and require some bushwhacking, but mostly I stay on the path.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery. Did you use it on your last hike?

Participant: No, I didn’t need it on the hike but used it while driving back.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old. On longer hikes, I might need to recharge. It’s also a precaution in case something happens and I need to keep my phone or flashlight powered.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record your hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less often because I had to take out the device to record. It made me more conscious of how I used it. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes, which could be interesting.

Interviewer: Did using the device take away from your experience in nature?

Participant: Somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband was using the same device, it felt natural. We reminded each other when to record. It felt a bit strange when other people saw me talking to myself, though.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay hiking?

Participant: Yes. On the last hike, we had to get back for an errand, so we checked the time to make sure we left in time.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Unfortunately, yes.

Interviewer: Could you hike without checking the time and still enjoy it?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time when you have an appointment would be more stressful. But if I don’t have somewhere to be, I get a better experience without worrying about time. Sometimes I check the time just to be aware of the heat of the day.

Interviewer: You used the map a few times on a trail you know well. Why?

Participant: The trail is less used and sometimes overgrown, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS connection, so I check to ensure it’s recording properly.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating.

Interviewer: We appreciate your time. We’ll reach out when the gift card is ready.

Participant: Thank you. Have a good evening.

Interviewer: You too. Goodbye.

Participant: Bye.

## Summary
**Short:** The participant views hiking as a beneficial outdoor activity that promotes physical fitness, mental relaxation, and creative inspiration through photography. They prepare carefully with essential gear and technology like a phone, extra battery, and Fitbit, and balance hiking alone and with friends to enjoy both solitude and social bonding. They prefer established trails, appreciate seasonal changes, and use apps like Strava for safety and connection. While technology sometimes distracts, it also enhances their experience by helping track progress and preserve memories.

**Long:** The participant describes hiking as a multifaceted activity that supports their physical health, mental well-being, and artistic pursuits. They hike frequently, mostly on weekends from spring to fall, splitting time between familiar and new trails. Preparation includes layered clothing, food, first aid, hiking poles, and technology such as a phone with GPS and an extra battery, along with a Fitbit for fitness tracking. Hiking alone allows for reflection and close observation of nature, while hiking with friends offers social interaction without significantly detracting from relaxation. They prioritize safety by sticking to known trails, sharing GPS location via Strava, and avoiding unfamiliar paths alone. The participant values the seasonal variety in hiking experiences and prefers challenging hikes when time permits. Although technology like cameras and recording devices occasionally distracts from immersion in nature, it also aids in capturing memories and monitoring progress. Time awareness can sometimes limit the hiking experience, but the participant balances this with practical needs. Overall, hiking is a meaningful, well-prepared activity that blends physical exercise, mental refreshment, social connection, and creative expression.

# Interview Analysis

## Transcript
Interviewer: Before we get into your hiking experience last week, what does hiking generally mean to you?

Participant: I enjoy hiking as a way to do things that are good for me. I like being outdoors, away from the computer and work, to relax and think without constant noise. I enjoy seeing different plants, animals, and views. I also like to paint, so I often take pictures on hikes to paint later.

Interviewer: How do you prepare for your hikes?

Participant: I bring enough food, water, first aid supplies, layers of clothes, hiking poles, boots, and a backpack. I make sure my phone is fully charged and usually bring an extra battery pack for my phone or flashlight, just in case.

Interviewer: What benefits do you gain from hiking?

Participant: It helps me get physically fitter, stronger, and improves my cardio health. I enjoy hiking with others because it’s a good time to talk and bond. Mentally, I always feel refreshed, calmer, and ready to tackle other things after hiking.

Interviewer: When hiking with friends and chatting, does that distract you from feeling refreshed?

Participant: Sometimes I focus more on the conversation and might miss some sights, but usually the topics aren’t stressful, so it still helps me relax. We also have quiet periods during hikes, so it balances out.

Interviewer: What’s the difference between hiking alone and with friends?

Participant: When alone, I tend to think more and notice my surroundings better. Sometimes I hike without my phone or listen to podcasts. With friends, there’s more social interaction.

Interviewer: How often do you hike?

Participant: Almost every weekend when the weather is nice, from spring to fall, about 20 to 30 times a year.

Interviewer: How long do your hikes usually last?

Participant: These days, about two to three hours. In past years, I’ve done longer hikes up to six hours or more.

Interviewer: Do you hike the same places or different locations?

Participant: About half and half. I revisit some favorite spots yearly but also look for new places.

Interviewer: What motivates you to return to the same places?

Participant: I enjoy those places a lot, and they’re usually close by. I like seeing how the hike changes with the seasons and tracking my own progress over time.

Interviewer: Do seasonal changes affect your well-being when hiking?

Participant: Yes, I appreciate different things in each season. No one season is better, but each offers unique experiences.

Interviewer: When hiking alone, how do you handle safety concerns?

Participant: I take precautions by only hiking trails I know well, usually ones I’ve done with a group before. I stick to shorter, well-traveled trails where I’m likely to see other people. I use an app called Strava to share my GPS location with family and tell them when I expect to return.

Interviewer: Besides Strava, do you use other apps or devices to connect with nature or stay focused?

Participant: I use my phone camera to take pictures and a Fitbit watch to track steps and heart rate.

Interviewer: Why do you use the camera on hikes?

Participant: To capture nice views, interesting patterns, or animals so I can remember them and paint later. Also, to identify plants by taking pictures and looking them up afterward.

Interviewer: How do these pictures help you connect back to your hikes?

Participant: I upload them to Google Photos, which sends reminders of where and when I was. Looking back at pictures helps me recall the experience more vividly than just writing notes.

Interviewer: Why don’t you use other hiking devices?

Participant: The phone and Fitbit cover my needs. I’m not sure what else is out there, but I’d try something new if it seemed helpful.

Interviewer: Without apps like Strava and the camera, would you feel safe hiking alone?

Participant: Somewhat. I’d stick to shorter, busier trails and be out for less time. I wouldn’t feel confident going far without location tracking.

Interviewer: What are your preferences for hike difficulty and length?

Participant: I like challenging hikes. Time is the biggest factor. The longest I’ve done is about 16.5 miles. On a day off, I prefer longer hikes—eight miles or more. Otherwise, six or seven miles is typical.

Interviewer: Regarding your recent hike, I want to share your trail map. Is that okay?

Participant: Yes, that’s fine.

Interviewer: We noticed your GPS on Strava started after you had already been hiking for a while. Why?

Participant: I’m not sure. I thought I started it at the trailhead. Maybe I activated it late by mistake.

Interviewer: You didn’t use your device much during the hike. Why?

Participant: It’s a trail I’ve done several times, so I didn’t need to check the map often. Also, it was warm that day, so I moved quickly and didn’t stop much to take pictures.

Interviewer: When you check the time, do you use your Fitbit or phone?

Participant: Just the Fitbit if I’m only checking the time. I might see the time on my phone when I pick it up, but I prefer the watch for time checks.

Interviewer: You took only two pictures on this hike. Why so few?

Participant: Most of the hike was in the trees, so fewer photo opportunities. I took one at a lookout and another of a plant I wanted to identify.

Interviewer: How do you feel being in an environment far from human living spaces?

Participant: I really enjoy it. It’s alive with plants and animals, different sounds and smells. It’s very different from daily life, so I notice more about my surroundings.

Interviewer: Do you prefer hiking on established trails or making your own path?

Participant: I stick to established trails to help preserve the environment and for safety. Sometimes trails are overgrown, but I avoid going off-trail.

Interviewer: You mentioned carrying an extra battery pack. Did you use it on this hike?

Participant: No, I used it on the drive back but didn’t need it during the hike.

Interviewer: Why carry an extra battery?

Participant: My phone battery drains quickly since it’s old, so for longer hikes, I might need it. Also, as a precaution in case of emergencies, to keep my phone or flashlight charged.

Interviewer: Is an extra battery necessary?

Participant: Not for short hikes, but very useful for longer ones.

Interviewer: How was your experience using the device to record your hike? Did it distract you?

Participant: It didn’t make a huge difference. I actually checked my watch less because I didn’t want to interrupt recording. It made me more conscious of device use. It also made me think about keeping a hike journal or notes.

Interviewer: Did being conscious of device use detract from your nature experience?

Participant: Somewhat, yes.

Interviewer: How comfortable were you using the device while hiking with others?

Participant: Since my husband used the same device, it felt normal. We reminded each other when to record. But it felt a bit strange talking to myself when passing other hikers.

Interviewer: Did checking the time affect how long you wanted to stay out hiking?

Participant: Yes, especially on this hike because my husband had an errand, so we needed to leave on time.

Interviewer: Does checking the time distract from the wellness you seek in nature?

Participant: Yes, unfortunately.

Interviewer: Could you hike without checking the time and still enjoy the experience?

Participant: It depends. Not knowing the time with an appointment would be more stressful. But I get a better experience when I don’t have to worry about time. Sometimes I check time to gauge the heat of the day rather than to plan my return.

Interviewer: Why did you use the map on a trail you know well?

Participant: The trail is less used and overgrown in parts, so I check to make sure I’m on the right path. Also, my phone sometimes loses GPS signal, so I check to ensure it’s recording.

Interviewer: Thank you for participating. We will send you a gift card as compensation once it’s ready.

Participant: Sounds good. I enjoyed participating. Thank you.

Interviewer: Thank you. We’ll be in touch. Have a good evening.

Participant: You too. Goodbye.

Interviewer: Goodbye.
